bull_thesis: FIG is a post-IPO supply-overhang special situation, not a broken franchise. Figma IPO'd ~$33 in late July 2025, spiked to ~$142 intraday on a textbook IPO-mechanics retail trap, then collapsed ~81% off the high to ~$19 as four tranches of insider lock-up supply hit the tape. The franchise is excellent and REACCELERATING: $1.16B TTM revenue at ~41% growth, Q1'26 +46% YoY to $333M, EV/S ~7x versus a software peer average near 19x. The catalyst is EXHAUSTION of the forced seller: the Extended Lock-Up (54.1% of Class A, >$6B low-cost-basis) releases on a phased schedule of which 65% (three of four tranches) has ALREADY cleared across Q3'25-Q1'26. The FINAL ~35% (~77.7M shares) releases after Q2'26 earnings or Aug 31, 2026, whichever is first. Once that last structural seller is gone, the float can reprice toward fundamentals. With a 40%-grower trading at a deep discount to peers and the entire VC overhang flushed, a re-rate toward the ~$32 level (still below the ~$36.88 street avg target) is the win value. Downside is tightly bounded: live ~$19 sits only ~9% above the ~$17.50 floor.
bear_thesis: The load-bearing event is a supply RELEASE, not a value-resolving deal close. On/around Aug 31, 2026 the final ~77.7M low-cost-basis shares (>$6B notional) unlock and become freely sellable by VCs whose basis is a fraction of the current price. Forced selling typically PRECEDES any re-rate, so the dated catalyst is bearish-on-arrival and can pin or pierce the stock for weeks. There is no contractual payout, no CVR, no SoP-conglomerate gap, no merger terms — the $32 target is purely an analyst-multiple re-rate that the market is free to ignore. GAAP optics are ugly (net loss -$1.40B, EPS -$3.38) which keeps generalists away during the exact window supply hits. AI design-tool displacement is a genuine secular question for a design franchise. A disorderly insider liquidation or any growth wobble at the Q2'26 print could pierce the ~$17.50 floor and revisit/break the $16.60 52-wk low.
sop_bull: Overhang-cleared re-rate value ~$32. A 40-46%-growth SaaS franchise with the structural forced-seller exhausted should not trade at EV/S ~7x against peers ~19x. Applying even a conservative ~11-12x forward sales to ~$1.3-1.4B forward revenue, net of the dual-class discount, supports ~$32/share — still a discount to the ~$36.88 street average and well inside the $25-$63 analyst range. This is the win value if the Aug'26 unlock flushes and clears.
sop_bear: Washout floor ~$17.50 (52-wk low $16.60). Even haircutting the multiple to a punished ~6x EV/S to reflect a disorderly insider dump and slowing growth, a $1.16B-revenue, 40%-grower holds a multiple-on-growth base in the high-$16s to high-$17s. The floor is a technical/multiple floor, not net cash, so it is real but not bulletproof — a growth miss plus disorderly liquidation could pierce it.
sop_breakdown: Win value (overhang cleared, ~11-12x fwd sales re-rate): ~$32. Floor/loss value (disorderly unlock, punished ~6x multiple): ~$17.50. Probability-weight ~45% to the re-rate / ~55% to a slow-clearing or floor outcome (because the catalyst ADDS supply and the absorption is unconfirmable pre-event) => base-case SoP ~$24. Anchor: $1.16B TTM rev, +41-46% growth, EV/S 7x vs peers ~19x; street avg target $36.88 (range $25.25-$63).
risk_reward: Upside-to-target ~+67% (to $32) vs downside-to-floor ~-8.5% (to $17.50) from ~$19.12 live; dossier-stated SoP R:R ~3.0. Asymmetry is attractive on paper, BUT it is gated on a bearish-on-arrival supply event, so the realized R:R depends on entering AFTER the Aug'26 flush rather than ahead of it.
catalyst_summary: Forced-seller / post-IPO supply-overhang exhaustion. Extended Lock-Up (8-K, 2025-08-30) on 54.1% of Class A (>$6B low-cost-basis) releases in four tranches; 65% (three tranches) already cleared Q3'25-Q1'26. FINAL ~35% (~77.7M sh) unlocks after Q2'26 earnings or Aug 31, 2026, whichever first. Dated and binding, but it RELEASES supply (bearish-on-arrival) and the upside is an analyst-multiple re-rate, not a contractual payout — hence SOFT_EXTENDED / capitulation-watchlist, not a clean PENDING_HARD arb. Verified live ~$19.12 (06-22-2026), 52-wk low $16.60.
dated_milestone: Final insider lock-up release after Q2 2026 earnings or Aug 31, 2026 (whichever is first); ~77.7M shares (~35% of the 54.1% Class A block).
forcing_function: The Aug 31, 2026 / Q2'26-earnings final lock-up expiration — the last ~77.7M-share, >$6B low-cost-basis insider tranche becomes freely sellable. Once this final structural seller is exhausted, the overhang that has capped the float since IPO is gone, enabling a re-rate toward fundamentals.
consensus_delta: The street narrative is 'expensive money-loser still bleeding from the IPO collapse' — anchored on the -$1.40B GAAP loss and the IPO-mechanics crash, with a Hold consensus (~$36.88 avg target). What the street under-weights is that the supply overhang is mathematically self-extinguishing: 65% of the locked insider block already cleared across Q3'25-Q1'26, and the FINAL tranche unlocks Aug'26 — after which the structural forced seller that has capped the float since IPO is GONE. The market is pricing FIG at EV/S ~7x while a 40-46%-grower with a cleared overhang is worth a multiple far closer to the ~19x peer set. The mispricing is technical (IPO supply), not fundamental.
valley_of_death: The next 3-9 months ARE the valley: the Aug 31, 2026 / Q2'26 unlock dumps ~77.7M shares (>$6B) of sub-IPO-basis VC stock into the float. This is the trade's defining hazard — forced selling will likely pin or pressure the stock into and through the event, and a soft Q2'26 growth print landing in the same window could pierce the $17.50 floor toward the $16.60 low. No cash-burn maturity wall (it is a cash-rich, equity-funded SaaS), so the risk is purely the supply flush, not solvency.
positioning_washout: The mechanical seller IS the insider/VC block whose basis is a fraction of $19 — they sell into ANY liquidity regardless of valuation, which is exactly the washout that creates the entry. Conversely, IPO-chasing retail and momentum holders have already been flushed (81% off the high). The remaining forced-selling is the Aug'26 tranche; after it clears, the marginal seller disappears and the shareholder base resets to fundamentals-driven holders.
moderator_conclusion: FIG is a legitimate forced-seller exhaustion special situation with an excellent, reaccelerating franchise (+46% YoY Q1'26, EV/S 7x vs peers 19x) trapped under IPO supply mechanics. The asymmetry is genuine (R:R ~3.0; ~+67% to a $32 re-rate vs ~-8.5% to a $17.50 floor only ~9% below live, with the 52-wk low at $16.60 already largely realized). BUT — and this is decisive for the verdict — the load-bearing catalyst is a supply RELEASE on Aug 31, 2026 / Q2'26 earnings, i.e. it is BEARISH-on-arrival: >$6B of insider stock hits the tape BEFORE the re-rate it enables. You do not front-run $6B of forced supply. This is a WATCHLIST-FOR-CAPITULATION (B), not an aggressive entry (A): wait for the final Aug'26 tranche to flush, confirm an exhaustion base, then enter on the cleared overhang. Conviction 4 — the thesis is real, dated, and the downside is tightly bounded, but the timing demands patience to the specific Aug'26 flush. Value_conviction 4: even ignoring the catalyst, a 40%-grower at 7x sales versus a 19x peer set is genuine value with a real floor; the only thing capping it from 5 is the unconfirmable absorption of the final unlock and the manageable (not absent) AI-displacement secular question.
interrogator_dossier:
# FIG — Figma Inc — Interrogator Dossier (Event-Driven Special Situation)

**Frame:** This is NOT a quality/compounding judgment. FIG is a forced-seller / post-IPO supply-overhang special situation. The thesis is the SPREAD between the live price (~$19.12, 06-22-2026) and an overhang-cleared re-rate target (~$32), gated on the final insider lock-up clearing, with a downside floor anchored to the post-washout technical base (~$16.60 52-wk low / ~$17.50 floor). The business is excellent (40%+ growth, design-software franchise); the price is wrecked by IPO mechanics, not by a broken franchise.

## 1. THE EVENT — is it real, dated, binding?

REAL and DATED, but the catalyst polarity is supply-RELEASE, not supply-CLEARING-by-a-fixed-trigger. Figma IPO'd late July 2025 (~$33 IPO, spiked to ~$142 intraday, then collapsed ~81% off the high; down ~40% YTD). On 2025-08-30 the company struck an Extended Lock-Up Agreement covering 54.1% of Class A shares (the VC/insider low-cost-basis block, >$6B notional) with a PHASED release: 17.5% (~38.9M) after Q3'25 earnings (DONE); 20% (~44.4M) after 2025 year-end earnings (DONE); 27.5% (~61.1M) after Q1'26 earnings (DONE); remaining ~35% (~77.7M) after Q2'26 earnings OR 2026-08-31, whichever FIRST. The load-bearing forcing function is the final ~77.7M-share unlock around the Q2'26 print / Aug 31, 2026 — a HARD, DATED, binding corporate event (SEC-filed 8-K). Critically, the event ADDS supply; it is bearish-on-arrival. The bull thesis is 'the LAST overhang tranche clears, the forced-seller is exhausted, and the float reprices to fundamentals' — a capitulation/washout setup, not a deal-close arb.

## 2. THE VERIFIED DOWNSIDE FLOOR

Technical/washout floor, not an asset floor. 52-wk low $16.60; dossier floor $17.50. Real support: $1.16B TTM revenue growing ~41% (Q1'26 +46% YoY, $333M), EV/S ~7x vs software peers ~19x. On any reasonable forward-sales multiple, ~$17-18 is a defensible base for a 40%-grower; the float would have to assume the entire insider block dumps at once AND a growth break for the floor to pierce. Real but a multiple-on-growth floor, not net-cash.

## 3. RED FLAGS (event-specific)

Catalyst polarity is wrong for an 'aggressive entry' — the dated event RELEASES >$6B of low-cost-basis stock; forced selling PRECEDES the re-rate. No deal/fixed payout/CVR/SoP gap — the $32 target is an analyst-multiple re-rate (street avg $36.88, range $25.25-$63), not an event-locked value. Massive net loss (-$1.40B, EPS -$3.38) keeps generalists away during the supply window. Dual-class founder control makes public float a price-taker to the insider selling cadence.

## 4. GREEN FLAGS

The worst of the overhang is mathematically already behind (65% of the locked block cleared Q3'25-Q1'26; Aug'26 is the LAST tranche). Franchise intact and reaccelerating (+46% YoY Q1'26). EV/S 7x vs peers ~19x is a genuine post-washout discount on top-decile-growth SaaS. Live ~$19.12 sits only ~9% above the $17.50 floor — downside tightly bounded and largely realized.

## 5. CATALYST CLASSIFICATION

Dated (Aug 31, 2026 / Q2'26) and binding, but a supply-RELEASE that must be ABSORBED before the re-rate, with upside an analyst-multiple re-rate rather than a contractual payout. SOFT_EXTENDED / capitulation-watchlist, not a clean PENDING_HARD arb. Right posture: WAIT FOR THE FLUSH (the Aug unlock), then enter on a confirmed exhaustion base.

## 6. CAPITAL-ALLOCATION VERDICT (for the director)

Classify: PIVOTING (post-IPO repricing, franchise intact). The single most important thing the director must weigh: this is a forced-seller exhaustion trade whose load-bearing event ADDS supply on a fixed date — you are paid to wait for the Aug'26 unlock to flush, not to front-run it. Floor real (~$17.50), downside bounded (~9%), but the catalyst is bearish-on-arrival, so the asymmetry is a WATCHLIST (B), not an aggressive entry (A). Moat: NARROW design-collaboration franchise (network/switching costs); secular AI design-tool displacement is manageable, not terminal, since Figma is itself shipping AI features.

CREDIBILITY_SCORE: 3 | TRAJECTORY: PIVOTING | MOAT: NARROW | MOAT_TREND: STABLE | SECULAR_THREAT: manageable
skeptic_kill_fact: The load-bearing event is a SUPPLY RELEASE with no contractual payout — bearish-on-arrival, not a value-resolving close. The 8-K (2025-08-30) is confirmed verbatim (17.5%/20%/27.5% tranches done, final ~77.7M sh / ~35% on the earlier of Q2'26 earnings+2d or 2026-08-31), so the date is real, but the $32 target is PURE analyst-multiple hope with zero event-locked floor. Decisive disqualifier vs the bull's 'absorb-then-re-rate' mechanism: the three prior tranches already cleared across Q3'25-Q1'26 and did NOT produce a re-rate — FIG fell ~40% YTD and sits at/near its $16.60 52-wk low THROUGH the absorption, empirically falsifying the claim that exhausting the seller repaces the float. The final >$6B tranche is the largest and lowest-basis, so the single dated catalyst can pin or pierce the floor for weeks. This is a watchlist-for-capitulation (B), never a front-run.

catalyst.dated_milestone: Final insider lock-up release after Q2 2026 earnings or Aug 31, 2026 (whichever is first); ~77.7M shares (~35% of the 54.1% Class A block).
catalyst.binding_reason: Forced-seller / post-IPO supply-overhang exhaustion. Extended Lock-Up (8-K, 2025-08-30) on 54.1% of Class A (>$6B low-cost-basis) releases in four tranches; 65% (three tranches) already cleared Q3'25-Q1'26. FINAL ~35% (~77.7M sh) unlocks after Q2'26 earnings or Aug 31, 2026, whichever first. Dated and binding, but it RELEASES supply (bearish-on-arrival) and the upside is an analyst-multiple re-rate, not a contractual payout — hence SOFT_EXTENDED / capitulation-watchlist, not a clean PENDING_HARD arb. Verified live ~$19.12 (06-22-2026), 52-wk low $16.60.

bull_thesis: Figma is a high-quality cloud design-software business whose stock has been wrecked by plumbing, not fundamentals. Revenue is about $1.16 billion over the trailing twelve months and still growing roughly 41% (Q1 FY26 was up 46% to $333 million), the company holds around $1.6 billion of net cash with little or no debt, and it now throws off real free cash flow ($88.6 million in the first quarter of fiscal 2026, a 27% margin). Yet the shares have round-tripped from a post-IPO peak near $140 to about $17-19, down close to 80%, because a staggered insider lock-up (the contractual window barring pre-IPO holders from selling) has flooded the market with supply faster than buyers could absorb it. The bull case is mechanical and self-extinguishing: the worst is largely behind, with about 65% of the locked insider block already cleared across late 2025 and early 2026, and the final and largest release lands on August 31, 2026 (or the Q2 FY26 earnings date, whichever is first), when the remaining roughly 35% of the 54.1% Class A block - about $7 billion of stock at current prices - becomes free to sell. After that date there is no further contractual seller. Once that overhang clears and the float finds permanent homes, the deep discount the forced selling carved into the price (enterprise value to sales near 7x against a software peer set closer to 19x) can close, letting a 40%-plus grower re-rate toward roughly $32 - still below the average sell-side target. A second, independent lever is now live: activist Findell Capital is pushing cost discipline and product focus, and an AI product cycle (the Config 2026 launches) drove a guidance raise. For this to work, the wash-out must actually clear by autumn and growth must hold in the high-30s/40s rather than decelerate sharply.
bear_thesis: The load-bearing event is a supply RELEASE, not a value-resolving deal close - so it is bearish on arrival. On or around August 31, 2026 the final tranche of low-cost-basis insider stock (about $7 billion notional) unlocks and becomes freely sellable by venture funds whose basis is a fraction of the current price; they sell into any liquidity regardless of valuation. Forced selling typically PRECEDES any re-rate, so the dated catalyst can pin or pierce the stock for weeks. There is no contractual payout, no merger terms, no sum-of-the-parts gap - the $32 target is purely an analyst-multiple re-rate the market is free to ignore, and sell-side conviction is in fact FALLING (Wells Fargo cut its target from $42 to $36, RBC from $28 to $22). The published downside floor of $17.50 has already been pierced - the stock closed near $16.84 on June 25 - which means the market has shown it will trade straight through that level under supply pressure; a heavy August unlock into a weak tape could easily print $13-15. GAAP optics are ugly (net loss about -$1.40 billion, EPS -$3.38), keeping generalists away during the exact window supply hits. The only hard asset floor is cash, roughly $3 a share - nowhere near today's price. AI design-tool displacement is a genuine, if manageable, secular question. A disorderly insider liquidation or any growth wobble at the Q2 print could leave the stock dead money or lower for quarters.
consensus_delta: Where we disagree with the market: the street is treating Figma as a broken IPO and a decelerating money-loser, extrapolating the relentless price decline into the fundamentals and cutting targets (Wells Fargo $42 to $36, RBC $28 to $22). That conflates a mechanical supply problem with a demand problem. The business is still growing ~41% (Q1 +46%) with a 27% free-cash-flow margin and ~$1.6B net cash; the price action is overwhelmingly the lock-up overhang, not eroding demand, and that overhang is mathematically self-extinguishing - ~65% has already cleared and the final tranche unlocks in August, after which the structural forced seller is gone. The market prices the stock at ~7x sales while a 40%-grower with a cleared overhang belongs far closer to the ~19x peer set. Where we are NOT contrarian: the bears are right on timing - the biggest selling wave is still ahead and the $17.50 floor has already failed, so being early is expensive.
forcing_function: The August 31, 2026 / Q2 FY26-earnings final lock-up expiration - the last and largest low-cost-basis insider tranche (~$7B) becomes freely sellable. It is a hard, contractual calendar date with no discretion to slip. Once this final structural seller is exhausted, the overhang that has capped the float since the IPO is gone, enabling a re-rate toward fundamentals.
valley_of_death: The next three to nine months ARE the valley of death, and they are structural rather than financial. There is no cash-burn hump or debt wall - the company is free-cash-flow positive with ~$1.6B net cash and little debt. The risk is pure supply: earlier tranches already pressured the stock, and the final and largest block (~$7B of sub-IPO-basis venture stock) unlocks around August 31, 2026. Into a weak tape that wave can drive the stock through the already-broken $17.50 floor toward $13-15 before it stabilizes. A soft Q2 print or any growth-decel signal in the same window would amplify it. The named floor is soft and already pierced, so capital committed before the wash-out can sit underwater for a quarter or more.
positioning_washout: This is the core dynamic and it is still in progress, not finished. The mechanical sellers are the pre-IPO venture funds and employees whose basis is a fraction of $19; they sell into any liquidity regardless of valuation. The IPO-chasing retail and momentum holders have already been flushed (down ~80% from the high). The remaining forced supply is the final August 2026 tranche (~35% of the 54.1% block, ~$7B). The washout completes only after that block has cleared and the float finds permanent homes; until then the shareholder base is dominated by sellers, which is exactly why the clean entry is on the far side of August 31, not in front of it.
moderator_conclusion: Figma is a legitimate forced-seller exhaustion special situation with an excellent, reaccelerating franchise (+46% year-over-year in Q1, enterprise value to sales ~7x against peers ~19x, ~$1.6B net cash, free-cash-flow positive) trapped under post-IPO supply mechanics - the kind of mispricing this funnel exists to find. But the timing runs the wrong way for an immediate aggressive entry, and two verified facts cap conviction today. First, the load-bearing catalyst is a supply RELEASE on August 31, 2026: it pushes the price down or sideways under heavy volume, and the real profit comes from the absence of forced sellers in the months AFTER it clears, not from the date firing - you do not front-run ~$7B of forced supply. Second, the board's $17.50 floor has already been breached (stock ~$16.84 close, ~$18.68 intraday), so the downside is genuinely open to ~$13-15 on a heavy unlock; the floor is real but soft, backstopped by a 40%-grower with positive free cash flow and net cash rather than any contractual payout. The eventual setup is attractive: post-wash-out, a supply-free 40%-grower at ~$15-18 against a ~$32 target is clean 3:1-plus asymmetry. So the discipline is to wait for the specific event - the August final unlock to trade through and the supply to clear - then buy the de-risked name. Wait for the wash-out. Reconciled base-case fair value ~$24; conviction 3 and value-conviction 3 (the margin of safety to the soft, already-tested floor is thin, not generous, and sell-side targets are being cut, weakening the snap-back leg).
sop_breakdown: Probability-weighted between the event-win target and the soft, already-tested floor. Win value (overhang cleared, ~11-13x forward sales re-rate plus ~$1.6B net cash over ~528M shares): ~$33. Floor/loss value (disorderly final unlock into a weak tape, punished ~6-7x multiple, breached $17.50 level): ~$15. Weight ~45% to the re-rate and ~55% to a slow-clearing or floor outcome (the catalyst ADDS supply and absorption is unconfirmable before the event, and the stated floor has already failed) => base-case ~$24. Anchor: $1.16B trailing revenue, +41-46% growth, enterprise value to sales ~7x vs peers ~19x; net cash ~$1.6B; average sell-side target in the mid-$30s with cuts in progress.
risk_reward: From ~$18.7: upside to the ~$32 target is about +71%; downside to the verified soft floor near ~$15 is about -20%. Raw ratio is roughly 3.5:1 on paper - but it is timing-impaired, because the largest forced-selling wave (Aug 31) sits between today's price and the upside, AND the stated floor has already been pierced once. On a post-wash-out entry the same target against a ~$15 floor is clean asymmetry; in front of the unlock the effective near-term risk-reward is closer to even.
interrogator_dossier:
# FIG (Figma, Inc.) — Forensic Dossier: Forced-Seller Special Situation

*Event-driven review. The thesis here is not business quality — it is the gap between today's
depressed price and where the stock can trade once a mechanical, calendar-driven wave of insider
selling finishes clearing. Verified live on the web as of June 29, 2026.*

## 1. THE SETUP IN ONE PARAGRAPH

Figma is the cloud design-software company that went public in late July 2025 at $33 a share, spiked
toward $140 in the first days of trading, and has since round-tripped all the way back — closing
around $16.84 on June 25, 2026 and trading near $18.68 intraday on June 29, down roughly 40% this
year and down close to 80% from the post-listing peak. The collapse is not, in the first instance, a
business story: revenue is $1.16 billion over the trailing twelve months and still growing about 41%,
the company holds roughly $1.6 billion of cash and marketable securities with little or no debt, and
it now generates real free cash flow ($88.6 million in the first quarter of fiscal 2026, a 27%
margin). What has crushed the stock is plumbing — a staggered "lock-up" schedule (the window during
which pre-IPO insiders are contractually barred from selling) that releases shares in waves and floods
the market with supply faster than new buyers arrive. The event we are underwriting is the clearing of
that overhang: the final and largest release falls on August 31, 2026, when holders of roughly 54.1%
of the Class A shares — venture funds and employees, about $7 billion of stock at current prices —
become free to sell. The bet is that once that last forced-seller wave washes through, the structural
discount it has carved into the price can begin to close.

## 2. IS THE CATALYST REAL, DATED, AND BINDING?

Yes on all three, and this is the strongest part of the case. The dates are not analyst guesses; they
come from the company's own contractual lock-up architecture, disclosed in SEC filings:

- A standard 180-day lock-up expired January 27, 2026.
- On August 30, 2025 Figma signed an *extended* lock-up covering ~54.1% of Class A shares, with
  staggered quarterly releases tied to earnings dates running through mid-2026 (unlock tranches landed
  around September 30 and December 31, 2025, and March 31, 2026).
- The final remainder — the venture-capital block holding over half the shares — releases on
  **August 31, 2026.**

So the forcing event is a hard, dated, contractual one. There is no FDA panel, no acquirer, no
regulator whose discretion could slip the date. That removes the single biggest risk in event
investing — an undated or serially-extended catalyst. The catalyst is binding because it is a calendar
mechanic, not a decision.

## 3. THE CRITICAL NUANCE — THIS IS A SUPPLY DUMP, NOT A RE-RATING TRIGGER

Here is the honest complication, and it is the thing the director must weigh hardest. A normal special
situation has a catalyst that *lifts* the price on a date (a deal closes, a drug is approved). This
one is the opposite: August 31 is the date the *largest wave of selling becomes legal*. The event
itself pushes the price down, or at best sideways under heavy volume, in the days and weeks around it.
The profit does not come from the unlock firing — it comes from the *absence of forced sellers* in the
weeks and months *after* the overhang has cleared, once the float has found permanent homes and the
mechanical seller is finally gone. In other words, the asymmetric entry is on the *far side* of the
wash-out, not in front of it. Every prior unlock tranche in this schedule (September, December, March)
"contributed materially to selling pressure and downward price movement." There is no reason to expect
the biggest tranche to behave differently.

## 4. THE VERIFIED DOWNSIDE FLOOR — AND WHY IT IS SOFT

The Catalyst-Watch board carries a floor of $17.50. The problem, verified today, is that the stock is
*already trading at or below that level* (≈$16.84 close, ≈$18.68 intraday). A floor the price has
already pierced is not a floor — it is a level the market has demonstrated it will trade through under
supply pressure. The real backstops are:

- **Cash:** ~$1.6 billion of net cash on ~528 million shares is roughly $3 a share. That is a hard
  asset floor but a low one — it does not protect $17.
- **A fundamentals-based floor:** one published discounted-cash-flow model pegs fair value near
  **$19.70** even on cautious assumptions, and the company is free-cash-flow positive and growing 40%+.
  That argues the *business* is worth roughly today's price or a little more — which means the downside
  from here is bounded more by valuation support (~$15–18 on a bad-tape, decel scenario) than by a
  contractual payout. There is no deal price, cash-tender, or recovery value that hard-caps the loss.

So the floor is real in the sense that a 40%-grower with positive cash flow and net cash will not go to
zero — but it is *soft*: it sits right where the stock already trades, and a heavy August unlock into a
weak tape could easily print $13–15 before it stabilizes.

## 5. RED FLAGS AND GREEN FLAGS

🔴 The biggest forced-seller wave is still *ahead* (Aug 31), so the supply pressure that has defined the
stock all year is not yet spent — buying now means standing in front of it.
🔴 The stated floor has already broken; downside is genuinely open below it on a bad unlock.
🔴 GAAP net loss of about -$1.40 billion over the trailing year (heavily stock-compensation and
IPO-driven) makes the headline screen look ugly and can keep generalist buyers away.
🔴 Sell-side conviction is *falling*, not rising — Wells Fargo cut its target from $42 to $36, RBC from
$28 to $22 — which weakens the "snap-back to consensus" leg of the bull case.

🟢 The catalyst is genuinely hard-dated and contractual — no slippage risk.
🟢 The underlying business is high-quality, not cheap-and-bad: ~41% revenue growth, positive free cash
flow at a 27% margin, ~$1.6 billion net cash, an AI product cycle (Config 2026 launches) that lifted
guidance.
🟢 An activist (Findell Capital) is now engaged, pushing cost discipline and focus — a second,
independent source of potential upside.
🟢 The math of the overhang is self-extinguishing: after August 31 there is no further contractual
seller. The single force depressing the price has a known expiry.

## 6. CAPITAL-ALLOCATION VERDICT FOR THE DIRECTOR

The single most important thing to weigh: this is a real, hard-dated, binding catalyst — but it is a
forced-*selling* event, so the clean asymmetric entry is *after* August 31, 2026, not before it, and
the published floor has already been breached. The credible play is to wait for the final-unlock
wash-out to clear the decks, then buy the de-risked, supply-free name into a recovering business. Buying
in front of the largest unlock, below a floor that has already failed, is taking the supply risk for no
extra reward. Moat and secular-threat framing are largely beside the point on an event name like this —
the franchise is fine; the question is purely the supply mechanic and the entry timing.

CREDIBILITY_SCORE: 4 | TRAJECTORY: STABLE | MOAT: NARROW | MOAT_TREND: STABLE | SECULAR_THREAT: manageable
